planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in moist to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Rooper's red-hot poker blooms in autumn (September–November) rather than summer like most kniphofias, making it valuable for extending the season of dramatic red-hot poker flower spikes. It is somewhat more moist-tolerant than many species.

directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Rooper's red-hot poker provides a unique seasonal value among kniphofias — it blooms in autumn (September through November) rather than in summer like the majority of red-hot pokers, extending the season of dramatic, torch-like flower spikes deep into the fall border season when most summer perennials have finished. The flower spikes are globular to egg-shaped (more oval than the elongated cylinders of most kniphofias) and in the characteristic two-toned red-and-yellow combination — orange-red to scarlet upper florets fading to golden-yellow at the base — creating distinctive bicolored rounded torches on tall stems above the typical, strap-leaved rosettes. The fall blooming time coincides with the southward migration of hummingbirds along the Atlantic and Gulf coastal flyways, making these flower spikes valuable late-season hummingbird nectar sources. Outstanding at the back of the fall border, combined with ornamental grasses, late asters, and goldenrods in a late-season color composition. More moisture-tolerant than many kniphofias from drier South African habitats.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bell agapanthus is deciduous — dying back to ground level in winter — unlike the evergreen A. africanus. This deciduous habit allows it to tolerate harder winters than evergreen agapanthus. Mulch the roots in zones 6–7 in winter. Plants resent disturbance once established and may take 2–3 years after division to flower freely.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bell agapanthus is the most cold-hardy commonly available agapanthus, surviving reliably to zone 6 where its deciduous habit (dying completely to the ground in winter) provides protection against cold that the evergreen species lack. The flowers are typical agapanthus umbels — spherical heads of 20–50 individual, tubular-funnel bells on tall, upright scapes in mid-blue to soft blue-violet — but on a somewhat more compact plant than the large, evergreen A. africanus. The deciduous habit provides a clear spring emerging point that makes the plant easier to manage: when growth emerges in spring, it confirms winter survival and signals when to resume watering. Several white and blue cultivars are available, and it is one of the parent species of many modern cold-hardy agapanthus hybrids that have extended the genus to zone 6. Outstanding for the mixed summer perennial border in zones 6–10 where summer blue flowers and bold scape structure are needed.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in spring; autumn pineapple lily blooms in late summer through fall (August–October) and goes dormant for winter. In zones 7–8 mulch heavily after the first frost. In zones 6 and colder lift bulbs after frost and store dry and frost-free for the winter.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; autumn pineapple lily is one of the most compact and garden-practical pineapple lily species — a smaller plant than E. comosa or E. pallidiflora that blooms in the late summer and autumn period (August through October), extending the pineapple lily season beyond the summer-blooming species into the transition to fall. The characteristic pineapple lily flower arrangement is retained — a dense, cylindrical spike of individual star-shaped flowers topped by a rosette of leaf-like bracts creating the silhouette that inspires the pineapple common name — but on a more compact plant 12–18 inches tall in pure white to cream-white or greenish-white. The autumn bloom timing is particularly valuable in the late-season border. Like all pineapple lilies, it is strongly fragrant — each individual floret lightly sweet, the whole spike pleasantly aromatic in warm weather — and the succulent, broadly strap-shaped basal leaves are attractive throughout the growing season. Outstanding in containers, where the pot can be moved indoors when frost threatens.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs 6–8 inches deep in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; giant pineapple lily produces very large, thick bulbs that take 2–3 years to reach peak flowering size. In zones 7–8 mulch heavily in winter. Lift bulbs in zone 6 and colder. Ideal planted in large containers where the tropical effect can be moved to shelter in winter.

directions: Plant large bulbs in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; giant pineapple lily is the largest species in the genus — producing the most impressive, most boldly architectural pineapple lily display available. The strap-shaped, broadly spreading, glossy-green basal leaves can reach 3 feet long and create a large, bold, tropical rosette before the flower scape emerges in midsummer. The flower scapes, reaching 3–4 feet, carry thick, substantial, densely flowered cylindrical spikes of greenish-white to cream-white, star-shaped flowers surmounted by the characteristic leafy bract rosette that creates the unmistakable pineapple silhouette — but at a scale substantially larger than any other species in the genus. The whole display is sweetly fragrant, the flowers attracting bees throughout their midsummer bloom period. In large containers on a terrace or in a prominent border position, a well-established clump of E. pallidiflora creates an architectural tropical effect that is difficult to match with any other bulbous plant in temperate climates. The subspecies subsp. pallidiflora is the most commonly grown; subsp. pole-evansii reaches even greater size.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall or early spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; bloodroot thrives in the margins of ponds, streams, and bogs, and requires consistently moist to wet conditions that simulate its natural South African fynbos wetland habitat. It is not drought-tolerant. The rhizomes and roots bleed a vivid blood-red to orange-red latex when cut, responsible for the common name.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; bloodroot (not to be confused with the North American woodland wildflower Sanguinaria canadensis, also called bloodroot) is a tall, impressive South African wetland perennial producing large, pleated, sword-shaped, bright-green, fan-like leaves of distinctive form and in spring (October through January in the Southern Hemisphere, April through June in cultivation), tall, branching panicles of vivid, bright-yellow, six-petaled, star-shaped flowers carried well above the leaf fans. The flowers are produced in such abundance on the tall, airy panicle that a large, established clump in full bloom is an impressive sight — masses of golden-yellow flowers floating above the architectural pleated foliage. The common name refers to the vivid blood-red to orange-red latex that bleeds from any cut surface of the red-pigmented rhizomes — a distinctive characteristic of the genus. It is a naturally marshy-edge plant of the South African Cape fynbos wetlands and requires consistently moist to wet conditions, making it ideal for the margins of garden ponds and bog gardens in zones 8–11.
